meta.title: The Author's Stance
meta.subject: Rhetorical primitives — how a docent film argues, not what it argues about

scenes.title: The Author's Stance
scenes.tagline: Four scene types render where the author stands — not what they describe

beats.narration: A docent film visualizes a subject. But every film also takes a stance toward that subject — what to cite, what to set aside, what to argue against, what to leave open. Sprint C gives that stance four scene types of its own.
These are not diagram primitives. They render the editorial commitment, the way tension and big-idea already do. This film walks all four, in the order they sit in a film.
Epigraph opens. Concession draws the line. Objection answers a real counterposition. Provocation hands the open question to the viewer.

scenes.heading: Argue with the quote, do not decorate from it
scenes.quote: A theory which is not refutable by any conceivable event is non-scientific. Irrefutability is not a virtue of a theory but a vice.

beats.narration: An epigraph opens the film by anchoring it in a tradition. Popper is the right opening for a film about argument because his line is itself an argument about what counts as one.
The depth test for an epigraph is whether the rest of the film engages with the quote. If we never return to Popper, this opening was decoration. We return to him in the objection scene.

scenes.heading: What this film does not cover
scope: The four new rhetorical scene types — epigraph, concession, objection, provocation
Where each sits in the film's spine, and why
The depth contract each scene type carries
outOfScope: How the renderer typesets each scene at the pixel level
The broader cognitive-representation primitives like structure, compare, tension
The treatment knobs — crisp, sketch, whiteboard skins — which apply across scene types
scenes.reason: We argue narrow because the rhetorical primitives are the contribution; the rest already has its own films.

beats.narration: Every film that argues something narrow ought to draw the line. Most do not, because the spec author forgets to. The concession is the move that strengthens every other claim — by saying what the film chooses not to fight about.
On the left, what this film is for. On the right, set aside with a strike-through, three concrete things this film does not cover. Below them, the cut — the one-line reason the line falls here.
Notice the items on the right are named, not gestured at. A concession that says merely not relevant is a footnote. A concession that names what is set aside is a real boundary.

scenes.heading: Four moves the existing grammar did not carry

nodes.label: epigraph
nodes.sub: a cited authority opens the film

nodes.label: the author's stance
nodes.sub: what they cite · cut · answer · leave open

beats.narration: Here is the claim the four scene types make together. Each one is a stance move, not a subject move.
The diagram primitives describe the subject. The rhetorical primitives describe where the author stands. Both are visualization; the grammar needed both registers.
Tension and big-idea already lived here — they argue trade-offs and takeaways. These four round out the rest of the rhetorical-move vocabulary serious arguments use.

scenes.heading: Four scene types instead of one callout primitive

nodes.label: four scene types
nodes.sub: each with its own depth contract and position rule

nodes.label: one generic callout
nodes.sub: free-form prose overlay on any scene

nodes.label: the cost we pay
nodes.sub: twelve fields on Scene; 4 renderers; 4 depthcheck rules

nodes.label: the risk we live with
nodes.sub: scene-count inflation if every editorial move becomes a type

nodes.label: what the scenes buy
nodes.sub: a regex can audit four shapes; a callout fails the depth gate silently

beats.narration: Why four new scene types and not one generic callout overlay? We chose four scene types instead of one. The cost: 12 new fields on the scene type, 4 renderers, and 4 depthcheck rules — a fixed 16 percent rise in grammar surface area.
What we buy is a regex that can audit each shape. A callout would fail the depth gate silently — there is no contract on free-form prose.

The residual risk is scene-count inflation. If every editorial move becomes its own type, the grammar bloats. We accept it because the four moves we picked are load-bearing — tension and big-idea already proved the pattern.

scenes.heading: A real counterposition the film must answer
scenes.claim: Rhetorical scene types belong in the docent grammar alongside the cognitive-representation primitives.
scenes.objection: Rhetorical is not a category of visualization primitive — these scene types conflate prose with diagram, inflate the grammar's scene-type count, and substitute fixed templates for what text overlays would carry without the cost.
evidence: Popper's quote is text; an epigraph is just a typesetting choice
A concession could be a callout, an objection a paragraph
Promoting words to scenes inflates the grammar
scenes.refutation: Tension and big-idea already proved this objection wrong: each renders an editorial commitment as a structured visual, not free-form prose. Concession's two-column ledger, objection's three-panel rhetorical layout, provocation's typographic ellipsis — each is a fixed grammar a regex can audit, with depth rules a callout cannot enforce.

beats.narration: Returning to Popper. A theory that cannot be refuted by any conceivable event is not science. By that standard, a grammar that cannot be argued against is not a grammar.

Here is the serious objection. Rhetorical is not a category of visualization primitive. These scenes render words. A grammar should not promote prose to first-class scene status when callouts would carry the load.

The answer is that tension and big-idea already proved the objection wrong. Each renders an editorial commitment as a structured visual with a depth contract — not free-form prose, not a callout. The rhetorical primitives extend the same pattern.

scenes.heading: The open question this sprint hands off
scenes.unresolved: Whether a fifth rhetorical primitive — a reservation, recording what the film argues despite, rather than what it argues against — would carry the half of editorial commitment these four do not
scenes.why: The four primitives cover citing, cutting, answering, and handing off. The shape they do not cover is the explicit live-with — the trade-off the film commits to despite knowing the cost.
scenes.invitation: Author a draft spec that uses reservation against your hardest case and bring it to the next sprint review.

beats.narration: The right ending for this film is not a takeaway. It is a question handed off. The four primitives cover four rhetorical moves, but they do not cover all of them.
The shape they do not carry is the explicit live-with — the trade-off the film commits to despite knowing the cost. The next sprint may need a fifth scene type.
If you have a case that wants this shape, bring a draft. The grammar is not closed.
